metadata.description: Post-incident postmortem reports for Acme Corp

postmortems.title: Payments Service Connection Pool Exhaustion - January 8, 2026
postmortems.author: Sarah Chen
postmortems.summary: During a flash sale event, the payments service experienced connection pool exhaustion due to insufficient pool size for the traffic volume. This caused HTTP 500 errors on the checkout endpoint, affecting 2,340 users and resulting in $45,000 in lost revenue.

root_cause_analysis.direct_cause: PostgreSQL connection pool size (max_connections=20) was insufficient for the traffic volume during the flash sale event
contributing_factors: Flash sale traffic was 3x normal volume
No load testing performed with flash sale traffic patterns
Connection pool size was set during initial deployment and never reviewed
No monitoring alerts for connection pool utilization
root_cause_analysis.why_it_happened: The connection pool size was configured during initial deployment with conservative estimates. As the business grew and flash sales were introduced, the pool size was never re-evaluated. The flash sale on January 8th generated 3x normal traffic, exhausting the pool.

resolution.immediate_action: Increased connection pool size to 50, added connection timeout of 30s, implemented connection health checks
resolution.long_term_fix: Implemented connection pool monitoring, added auto-scaling based on pool utilization, established quarterly capacity reviews

action_items.description: Increase payments-service connection pool size to 50
Add connection pool utilization monitoring and alerts
Perform load testing with flash sale traffic patterns
Establish quarterly capacity review process
Implement connection pool auto-scaling

lessons_learned: Connection pool sizing must be reviewed as traffic patterns change
Load testing should include peak traffic scenarios like flash sales
Monitoring alerts for resource utilization prevent cascading failures
Conservative initial configurations need periodic re-evaluation
preventive_measures: Quarterly capacity reviews for all tier-1 services
Mandatory load testing before marketing campaigns
Connection pool utilization alerts at 70% and 85% thresholds
Auto-scaling based on connection pool metrics

postmortems.title: Order Service Database Deadlock - February 3, 2026
postmortems.author: Sarah Chen
postmortems.summary: Concurrent updates to the same order row from order-service and inventory-service caused database deadlocks, resulting in order creation failures and inventory update delays. The incident affected 3,400 users and caused $67,000 in lost revenue.

root_cause_analysis.direct_cause: Concurrent updates to the same order row from order-service and inventory-service caused a deadlock
contributing_factors: No row-level locking implemented
Queries executed in different order between services
No deadlock detection or retry logic
High concurrency during peak hours increased deadlock probability
root_cause_analysis.why_it_happened: Order-service and inventory-service both update the same order row during order processing. Without proper locking mechanisms, concurrent updates caused deadlocks. The issue was not caught in testing because test scenarios didn't simulate high concurrency.

resolution.immediate_action: Implemented row-level locking with SELECT FOR UPDATE, optimized query order, added deadlock detection and retry logic
resolution.long_term_fix: Redesigned order processing workflow to minimize concurrent updates, implemented optimistic locking for non-critical updates

action_items.description: Implement row-level locking with SELECT FOR UPDATE
Add deadlock detection and retry logic
Optimize query order across services
Add concurrency testing to CI/CD pipeline

lessons_learned: Concurrent database updates require proper locking mechanisms
Query execution order matters in multi-service architectures
Deadlock detection and retry logic prevents cascading failures
Concurrency testing is essential for high-traffic services
preventive_measures: Mandatory row-level locking for shared resources
Standardized query ordering across services
Concurrency testing in CI/CD pipeline
Database deadlock monitoring and alerting

postmortems.title: Payments Service Connection Pool Exhaustion (Recurrence) - March 15, 2026
postmortems.author: Sarah Chen
postmortems.summary: The payments service experienced connection pool exhaustion for the second time, despite the fix from INC-001. The previous fix (increasing pool size to 50) was insufficient for the new traffic pattern caused by a partner integration that increased load by 3x. This incident affected 5,600 users and caused $98,000 in lost revenue.

root_cause_analysis.direct_cause: Connection pool size increase from INC-001 (to 50) was insufficient for the new traffic pattern caused by partner integration
contributing_factors: Partner integration increased traffic by 3x
No capacity review before partner integration launch
Connection pool monitoring alerts were not tuned for new traffic patterns
No circuit breaker to prevent cascading failures to order-service
root_cause_analysis.why_it_happened: The fix from INC-001 addressed the immediate issue but didn't account for future traffic growth. When the partner integration launched, traffic increased by 3x, overwhelming the connection pool again. The capacity review process established after INC-001 was not followed before the partner integration launch.

resolution.immediate_action: Increased connection pool to 100, implemented connection pool monitoring, added circuit breaker for payment endpoints, deployed read replicas for query offloading
resolution.long_term_fix: Implemented dynamic connection pool sizing based on traffic patterns, established mandatory capacity reviews for all integrations, added circuit breakers to all tier-1 services

action_items.description: Increase connection pool size to 100
Add circuit breaker for payment endpoints
Deploy read replicas for query offloading
Implement dynamic connection pool sizing
Establish mandatory capacity review for integrations
Add circuit breakers to all tier-1 services

lessons_learned: Capacity fixes must account for future growth, not just current needs
Partner integrations require mandatory capacity reviews
Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures between services
Dynamic resource sizing is more resilient than static configuration
Post-incident action items must be enforced, not just established
preventive_measures: Mandatory capacity review before any integration launch
Dynamic connection pool sizing based on traffic patterns
Circuit breakers on all tier-1 service dependencies
Read replicas for all high-traffic database queries
Quarterly capacity reviews with traffic growth projections
postmortems.follow_up_notes: This is the second occurrence of the same root cause. The fix from INC-001 was insufficient. We need to ensure all action items from postmortems are actually completed and enforced. The capacity review process was established but not followed.

postmortems.title: Deployment Rollback - Breaking API Change - April 18, 2026
postmortems.author: Sarah Chen
postmortems.summary: A breaking API change (removal of a deprecated field) was deployed without proper contract testing, causing 50% error rates on user endpoints and mobile app crashes. The incident affected 12,000 users and caused $45,000 in lost revenue.

root_cause_analysis.direct_cause: Breaking API change deployed without proper contract testing. Mobile app still depended on the removed field.
contributing_factors: No API contract tests in CI/CD pipeline
Deprecated field marked for removal but mobile app not updated
No API versioning strategy
Insufficient pre-deployment testing with mobile app
No canary deployment or gradual rollout
root_cause_analysis.why_it_happened: The deprecated field was marked for removal in the API documentation, but the mobile app team was not aware of the timeline. The field was removed in deployment v2.5.0 without checking mobile app dependencies. No contract tests existed to catch this breaking change.

resolution.immediate_action: Rolled back deployment to v2.4.9, restored API compatibility
resolution.long_term_fix: Added API contract tests to CI/CD pipeline, implemented API versioning strategy, established deprecation process with 90-day notice

action_items.description: Add API contract tests to CI/CD pipeline
Implement API versioning strategy
Establish deprecation process with 90-day notice
Add mobile app integration tests to CI/CD
Implement canary deployment strategy

lessons_learned: Breaking API changes require comprehensive contract testing
Deprecation process must include all dependent teams
Mobile app dependencies must be tracked and tested
Canary deployments prevent widespread impact from breaking changes
API versioning is essential for backward compatibility
preventive_measures: Mandatory API contract tests in CI/CD pipeline
90-day deprecation notice for all breaking changes
API versioning with backward compatibility guarantees
Mobile app integration tests before deployment
Canary deployment for all production releases

postmortems.title: Kubernetes Pod OOM Kills During Traffic Spike - June 1, 2026
postmortems.author: Sarah Chen
postmortems.summary: During a marketing campaign traffic spike, multiple pods experienced OOM kills due to insufficient memory limits. HPA was not configured for memory-based scaling, only CPU. The incident affected 15,000 users and caused $89,000 in lost revenue.

root_cause_analysis.direct_cause: Memory limits set too low for traffic spike during marketing campaign. HPA not configured for memory-based scaling.
contributing_factors: Memory limits set during initial deployment and never reviewed
HPA configured only for CPU, not memory
No memory usage monitoring or alerts
Marketing campaign traffic not accounted for in capacity planning
No load testing with marketing campaign traffic patterns
root_cause_analysis.why_it_happened: Memory limits were set conservatively during initial deployment. As the application grew and marketing campaigns drove traffic spikes, memory usage increased beyond limits. HPA was only configured for CPU scaling, so pods couldn't scale based on memory pressure.

resolution.immediate_action: Increased memory limits by 50%, configured HPA for memory-based scaling, added memory usage monitoring and alerts
resolution.long_term_fix: Implemented comprehensive resource monitoring, established capacity review process for marketing campaigns, added memory profiling to CI/CD

action_items.description: Increase memory limits by 50%
Configure HPA for memory-based scaling
Add memory usage monitoring and alerts
Establish capacity review for marketing campaigns
Add memory profiling to CI/CD pipeline

lessons_learned: Resource limits must be reviewed as application and traffic patterns evolve
HPA should scale on both CPU and memory for comprehensive protection
Marketing campaigns require mandatory capacity reviews
Memory monitoring and alerting prevent OOM kills
Load testing should include all traffic spike scenarios
preventive_measures: Quarterly resource limit reviews for all services
HPA configured for both CPU and memory scaling
Mandatory capacity review before marketing campaigns
Memory usage alerts at 70% and 85% thresholds
Load testing with marketing campaign traffic patterns

postmortems.title: Cascading Failure from Search Service Memory Leak - June 28, 2026
postmortems.author: Sarah Chen
postmortems.summary: A memory leak in the search service caused gradual degradation over 3.5 hours. Dependent services (product-service, order-service) waited for search responses, causing cascading timeouts across the platform. The incident affected 25,000 users and caused $156,000 in lost revenue, making it the most severe incident in company history.

root_cause_analysis.direct_cause: Memory leak in search service caused gradual degradation. Dependent services waited for search responses, causing cascading timeouts.
contributing_factors: No circuit breakers on dependent services
No timeout budgets implemented
Memory leak not detected by monitoring
No graceful degradation when search is unavailable
Search service memory limits too high, delaying OOM kill
No canary deployment to catch memory leaks early
root_cause_analysis.why_it_happened: A recent deployment introduced a memory leak in the search service. Memory usage gradually increased over 3.5 hours, causing slower query responses. Dependent services (product-service, order-service) had no timeout budgets or circuit breakers, so they waited indefinitely for search responses. This caused cascading timeouts across the platform.

resolution.immediate_action: Fixed memory leak in search service, added circuit breakers to all dependent services, implemented timeout budgets, deployed search service with increased memory limits
resolution.long_term_fix: Implemented comprehensive circuit breaker strategy across all services, established timeout budget standards, added memory leak detection to CI/CD, deployed chaos engineering tests

action_items.description: Fix memory leak in search service
Add circuit breakers to all dependent services
Implement timeout budgets across all services
Add memory leak detection to CI/CD pipeline
Implement graceful degradation when search is unavailable
Deploy chaos engineering tests for cascading failures

lessons_learned: Circuit breakers are essential to prevent cascading failures
Timeout budgets prevent indefinite waits on dependent services
Memory leak detection must be part of CI/CD pipeline
Graceful degradation is critical for service resilience
Cascading failures have exponential impact on users and revenue
Memory limits should be tuned to trigger OOM before causing cascading issues
preventive_measures: Mandatory circuit breakers on all service dependencies
Timeout budgets enforced across all service calls
Memory leak detection in CI/CD pipeline
Graceful degradation strategies for all critical paths
Chaos engineering tests for cascading failure scenarios
Quarterly resilience reviews for all tier-1 services
postmortems.follow_up_notes: This is the most severe incident in company history. The cascading nature of the failure amplified the impact significantly. We need to prioritize circuit breakers and timeout budgets across all services. Several action items from previous postmortems (like circuit breakers from PM-003) were not completed - we need better tracking and enforcement of postmortem action items.
